stimulus:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

  • Harper's Weekly magazine began publication in 1857.
  • The magazine covered politics, current events, and illustration.
  • In 2018, historian Mei-Ling Carter published the book Frontline Reporters of "Harper's Weekly".
  • The book examines women correspondents whose dispatches appeared in the magazine during the 1860s.
  • One reporter featured is Sarah Talbot, whose Civil War dispatches were widely read.
stem: The student wants to introduce Mei-Ling Carter's book to an audience already familiar with Harper's Weekly. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

answerOptions.content: Mei-Ling Carter's Frontline Reporters of "Harper's Weekly" examines women correspondents whose dispatches appeared in the magazine during the 1860s.
A magazine called Harper's Weekly, referred to in Mei-Ling Carter's book title, began publication in 1857.
Frontline Reporters of "Harper's Weekly" highlights contributors to Harper's Weekly, the magazine first published in 1857 that covered politics, current events, and illustration.
A reporter discussed by Mei-Ling Carter is Sarah Talbot, whose Civil War dispatches appeared in Harper's Weekly.

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer because the audience already familiar with Harper's Weekly doesn't need a long magazine history but does benefit from a clear statement of the book's subject paired with a light reminder of the publication.

Choice A doesn't connect the book to the audience's prior knowledge. Choice B over-explains the magazine. Choice D narrows to a single subject of the book.
